From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Restrictions on using GPS in China

Under Chinese law, the use of [geographic information in the People's Republic of China](/wiki/Geographic_information_systems_in_China "Geographic information systems in China") is restricted to entities that have special authorization from the administrative department for surveying and mapping under the [State Council](/wiki/State_Council_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "State Council of the People's Republic of China").[[1]](#cite_note-law-1) Consequences of the restriction include fines for unauthorized surveys, lack of [geotagging](/wiki/Geotagging "Geotagging") information on many cameras when the [GPS](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System") chip detects a location within China, and incorrect alignment of street maps with satellite maps in various applications.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Chinese lawmakers said that these restrictions are to "safeguard the security of China's geographic information".[[3]](#cite_note-Reuters-2017-3) Song Chaozhi, an official of the [State Bureau of Surveying and Mapping](/wiki/State_Bureau_of_Surveying_and_Mapping "State Bureau of Surveying and Mapping"), said "foreign organizations who wish to carry out mapping or surveying work within China must make clear that they will not touch upon state secrets or endanger state security".[[3]](#cite_note-Reuters-2017-3) Critics outside of China point out that the laws close critical sectors of the Chinese economy to foreign companies, and assist with cracking down on dissent.[[3]](#cite_note-Reuters-2017-3)

## Legislation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Restrictions_on_geographic_data_in_China&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Legislation")]

### Surveying

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Restrictions_on_geographic_data_in_China&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Surveying")]

According to articles 7, 26, 40 and 42 of the *Surveying and Mapping Law of the People's Republic of China*, private surveying and mapping activities have been illegal in mainland China since 2002. The law prohibits:[[4]](#cite_note-4)

> publishing, without authorization, significant geographic information and data concerning the territorial air, land and waters, as well as other sea areas under the jurisdiction of the People's Republic of China.

— The National Administration of Surveying, Mapping and Geoinformation of China, Surveying and Mapping Law of the People's Republic of China

Article 1 says:

> This Law is enacted to strengthen the administration of the surveying and mapping undertaking, promote its development and ensure that it renders service to development of the national economy, the building up of national defence, and progress of the society.[[5]](#cite_note-en.nasg.gov.cn-5)

Fines range from 10,000 to 500,000 [CNY](/wiki/Renminbi "Renminbi") (US$1550–77519). Foreign individuals or organizations that wish to conduct surveying must form a Chinese-foreign joint venture.[[1]](#cite_note-law-1)

Between 2006 and 2011, the authorities pursued around 40 illegal cases of mapping and surveying.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The media has reported on other cases of unlawful surveys:

* March 7, 2007 — Japanese and Korean scholars fined; joint-venture Weihai hired foreign surveyors without approval from the government[[7]](#cite_note-7)
* March 25, 2008 — China's [State Bureau of Surveying and Mapping](/wiki/State_Bureau_of_Surveying_and_Mapping "State Bureau of Surveying and Mapping") cracks down on some of the 10,000 websites that publish maps in China, most without authorization.[[8]](#cite_note-8)
* January 6, 2009 — Chinese authorities fine UK students for "illegal map-making activities".[[9]](#cite_note-9)
* 2010 — Chinese authorities to crack down on the unregistered or illegal among 42,000 online map providers, targeting incorrect information and leaks of sensitive information involving state secrets. New standards require all Internet map providers to keep servers storing map data inside China.[[10]](#cite_note-10)
* March 14, 2014 — Coca-Cola is accused of illegal mapping.[[11]](#cite_note-motherboard-11)

As a consequence, major digital camera manufacturers including Panasonic, Leica, FujiFilm, Nikon and Samsung restrict location information within China.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

[OpenStreetMap](/wiki/OpenStreetMap "OpenStreetMap"), the [crowdsourced](/wiki/Crowdsourcing "Crowdsourcing") project to assemble a map of the world, advises that "private surveying and mapping activities are illegal in China".[[13]](#cite_note-osm-pjcn-legal-13)

### Map content

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Restrictions_on_geographic_data_in_China&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Map content")]

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Wikisource-logo.svg/40px-Wikisource-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikisource-logo.svg)

Chinese [Wikisource](/wiki/Wikisource "Wikisource") has original text related to this article:

**[Category:PRC maps](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/zh:%E5%88%86%E7%B1%BB:%E4%B8%AD%E5%8D%8E%E4%BA%BA%E6%B0%91%E5%85%B1%E5%92%8C%E5%9B%BD%E5%9C%B0%E5%9B%BE "s:zh:分类:中华人民共和国地图")**

Chinese law and regulations also rule on the contents of any published map:

* The "Certain Provisions on the Display of Public Map Content" (Chinese: 公开地图内容表示若干规定) of 2003 by the [Bureau of Surveying and Mapping](/wiki/Bureau_of_Surveying_and_Mapping "Bureau of Surveying and Mapping") (BSM) prohibits the representation of sensitive information such as airports (except those specially listed), military bases, and waterway depths. It also describes the handling and naming of disputed territories and former [Qing](/wiki/Qing_China "Qing China") territories ceded to [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia").[[14]](#cite_note-14)
* The "Supplementary rules on the content presentation of public maps, trial version" (Chinese: 公开地图内容表示补充规定（试行）) of 2009 from the BSM additionally prohibits, among other things:[[15]](#cite_note-15)
  + Locations having a precision of finer than 50 metres (55 yd) and the use of grids finer than 100 metres (110 yd) for elevation data.
  + Showing the locations of key infrastructure (power, dams, weather stations) and public safety installations (prisons, mandatory drug rehab facilities).
  + Showing the internal structures of airports and ferry ports.
  + Showing the weight and height limits of bridges, roads, and tunnels; showing the material of roads.
* The "Map management regulation" (Chinese: 地图管理条例) of 2015 from the State Council mandates that all Internet maps must be stored in mainland China, among other rules about national security. The law also details punishments, some of which criminal, for violations.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

In 2016, a large-scale search by Chinese law enforcement found 253 types of problematic paper maps and 1000 problematic online map websites, most pertaining to the depiction of [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") and [9-dash line](/wiki/9-dash_line "9-dash line").[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Coordinate systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Restrictions_on_geographic_data_in_China&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Coordinate systems")]

| * [v](/wiki/Template:Geodesy "Template:Geodesy") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Geodesy "Template talk:Geodesy") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Geodesy "Special:EditPage/Template:Geodesy") |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/PRcoords_Cheatsheet.pdf/page1-250px-PRcoords_Cheatsheet.pdf.jpg)](/wiki/File:PRcoords_Cheatsheet.pdf)

JavaScript implementation of coordinate "processing" methods used in China

Chinese regulations require that approved map service providers in China use a specific coordinate system, called GCJ-02 (colloquially *Mars Coordinates*). [Baidu Maps](/wiki/Baidu_Maps "Baidu Maps") uses yet another coordinate system, BD-09,[[18]](#cite_note-bd-09-18)[[19]](#cite_note-baidu-map-api-coordinate-conversion-19) which seems to be based on GCJ-02.[[20]](#cite_note-caijun-20)

> Technical spatial processing must be applied to electronic navigational maps prior to publication, sales, redistribution, and usage.

— GB 20263―2006 "[Basic security processes for electronic navigational maps](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/zh:GB_20263-2006_%E5%AF%BC%E8%88%AA%E7%94%B5%E5%AD%90%E5%9C%B0%E5%9B%BE%E5%AE%89%E5%85%A8%E5%A4%84%E7%90%86%E6%8A%80%E6%9C%AF%E5%9F%BA%E6%9C%AC%E8%A6%81%E6%B1%82 "s:zh:GB 20263-2006 导航电子地图安全处理技术基本要求")", 4.1

### GCJ-02

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Restrictions_on_geographic_data_in_China&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: GCJ-02")]

**GCJ-02** ([Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 地形图非线性保密处理算法; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Dìxíng tú fēixiànxìng bǎomì chǔlǐ suànfǎ*; lit. 'Topographic map non-linear confidentiality algorithm')[[21]](#cite_note-cpc_chengming-21) is a [geodetic datum](/wiki/Geodetic_datum "Geodetic datum") used by the Chinese [State Bureau of Surveying and Mapping](/wiki/State_Bureau_of_Surveying_and_Mapping "State Bureau of Surveying and Mapping"), and based on [WGS-84](/wiki/WGS-84 "WGS-84").[[22]](#cite_note-22) It uses an obfuscation algorithm[[23]](#cite_note-23) which adds apparently random offsets to both the latitude and longitude, with the alleged goal of improving national security.[[20]](#cite_note-caijun-20)[[24]](#cite_note-24) There is a license fee associated with using this mandatory algorithm in China.[[21]](#cite_note-cpc_chengming-21)

A marker with GCJ-02 coordinates will be displayed at the correct location on a GCJ-02 map. However, the offsets can result in a 100–700 meter error from the actual location if a WGS-84 marker (such as a GPS location) is placed on a GCJ-02 map, or vice versa. The [Google Maps](/wiki/Google_Maps "Google Maps") street map is offset by 50–500 meters from its satellite imagery.[[11]](#cite_note-motherboard-11)[[25]](#cite_note-25) [Yahoo! Maps](/wiki/Yahoo!_Maps "Yahoo! Maps") also displayed the street map without major errors when compared to the satellite imagery.[[26]](#cite_note-yahoo-map-26) MapQuest overlays OpenStreetMap data perfectly as well.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Despite the secrecy surrounding the GCJ-02 obfuscation, several open-source projects exist that provide conversions between GCJ-02 and WGS-84, for languages including [C#](/wiki/C_Sharp_(programming_language) "C Sharp (programming language)"),[[28]](#cite_note-leak-28) [C](/wiki/C_(programming_language) "C (programming language)"), [Go](/wiki/Go_(programming_language) "Go (programming language)"), [Java](/wiki/Java_(programming_language) "Java (programming language)"), [JavaScript](/wiki/JavaScript "JavaScript"), [PHP](/wiki/PHP "PHP"),[[29]](#cite_note-evil-gh-29) [Python](/wiki/Python_(programming_language) "Python (programming language)"),[[30]](#cite_note-30) [R](/wiki/R_(programming_language) "R (programming language)"),[[20]](#cite_note-caijun-20) and [Ruby](/wiki/Ruby_(programming_language) "Ruby (programming language)").[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) They appear to be based on leaked code for the WGS to GCJ part.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Other solutions to the conversion involve interpolating coordinates based on [regression](/wiki/Regression_analysis "Regression analysis") from a data set of Google China and satellite imagery coordinates.[[34]](#cite_note-guilbot-reg-34) An attempt by Wu Yongzheng using [fast Fourier transform](/wiki/Fast_Fourier_transform "Fast Fourier transform") analysis gave a result much like the leaked code.[[35]](#cite_note-wu-regression-35)

From the leaked code,[[28]](#cite_note-leak-28)[[36]](#cite_note-36) GCJ-02 uses parameters from the [SK-42 reference system](/wiki/SK-42_reference_system "SK-42 reference system"). The parameters were used to calculate lengths of one degree of [latitude](/wiki/Latitude#Length_of_a_degree_of_latitude "Latitude") and [longitude](/wiki/Longitude#Length_of_a_degree_of_longitude "Longitude"), so that offsets in meters previously calculated can be converted to degrees for the WGS-84 input coordinates.

### BD-09

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Restrictions_on_geographic_data_in_China&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: BD-09")]

Main article: [Baidu Maps § Coordinate system](/wiki/Baidu_Maps#Coordinate_system "Baidu Maps")

BD-09 is a geographic coordinate system used by [Baidu Maps](/wiki/Baidu_Maps "Baidu Maps"), adding further obfuscation to GCJ-02 "to better protect users' privacy".[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[19]](#cite_note-baidu-map-api-coordinate-conversion-19) Baidu provides an API call to convert from Google or GPS (WGS-84), GCJ-02, BD-09, [MapBar](/w/index.php?title=MapBar&action=edit&redlink=1 "MapBar (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%9B%BE%E5%90%A7 "zh:图吧")] or [51ditu](/w/index.php?title=51ditu&action=edit&redlink=1 "51ditu (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%81%B5%E5%9B%BE "zh:灵图")] coordinates into Baidu or GCJ-02 coordinates.[[38]](#cite_note-baidu-api-manual-38)[[18]](#cite_note-bd-09-18) As required by local law,[[38]](#cite_note-baidu-api-manual-38) there is no API to convert into WGS-84, but open source implementations in R[[20]](#cite_note-caijun-20) and various other languages[[29]](#cite_note-evil-gh-29) exist.

### Reverse transformation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Restrictions_on_geographic_data_in_China&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Reverse transformation")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | Parts of this article (those related to the actual algorithm, which has now been made public through leaks) need to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(November 2025)* |

GCJ-02 appears to use multiple high-frequency noises of the form ![{\displaystyle 20n\sin {}(180k\times lat_{rad})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f9c44aeff167b5337565a1a79ece6793563286bd), effectively generating a [transcendental equation](/wiki/Transcendental_equation "Transcendental equation") and thus eliminating analytical solutions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] However, the open-source "reverse" transformations make use of the properties of GCJ-02 that the transformed coordinates are not too far from WGS-84 and are mostly monotonic related to corresponding WGS-84 coordinates:[[39]](#cite_note-fengzee-39)[[20]](#cite_note-caijun-20)

```
from typing import Callable

# Represent coordinates with complex numbers for simplicity
coords = complex
# Coords-to-coords function
C2C = Callable[[coords], coords]

def rev_transform_rough(bad: coords, worsen: C2C) -> coords:
    """Roughly reverse the ``worsen`` transformation.

    Since ``bad = worsen(good)`` is close to ``good``,
    ``worsen(bad) - bad`` can be used to approximate ``bad - good``.

    First seen in eviltransform.
    """
    return bad - (worsen(bad) - bad)

def rev_transform(bad: coords, worsen: C2C) -> coords:
    """More precisely reverse the ``worsen`` transformation.

    Similar to ``rev_transform_rough``,
    ``worsen(a) - worsen(b)`` can be used to approximate ``a - b``.

    First seen in geoChina/R/cst.R (caijun 2014).
    Iteration-only version (without rough initialization) has been known
    since fengzee-me/ChinaMapShift (November 2013).
    """
    eps = 1e-6
    wgs = bad
    improvement = 99 + 99j  # dummy value

    while abs(improvement) > eps:
        improvement = worsen(wgs) - bad
        wgs = wgs - improvement
    return wgs
```

The rough method is reported to give some 1~2 meter accuracy for wgs2gcj,[[29]](#cite_note-evil-gh-29) while the exact ([fixed point iteration](/wiki/Fixed_point_iteration "Fixed point iteration")) method is able to get "centimeter accuracy" in two calls to the forward function.[[note 1]](#cite_note-40)[[40]](#cite_note-gh_86ae326-41)[[39]](#cite_note-fengzee-39) The BD-to-GCJ code works in a manner much like the rough method, except that it removes the explicitly-applied constant shift of ~20 seconds of arc on both coordinates first and works in polar coordinates like the forward function does.[[20]](#cite_note-caijun-20)

The establishment of working conversion methods both ways largely renders obsolete datasets for deviations mentioned below.[[41]](#cite_note-Corr-42)

## GPS shift problem

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Restrictions_on_geographic_data_in_China&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: GPS shift problem")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/b/be/Google.com_Maps_in_China_coordinate_system_misalignment.png/250px-Google.com_Maps_in_China_coordinate_system_misalignment.png)](/wiki/File:Google.com_Maps_in_China_coordinate_system_misalignment.png)

Google Maps displays satellite imagery using the WGS-84 coordinate system, and street maps using the GCJ-02 datum

The China GPS shift (or offset) problem is a class of issues stemming from the difference between the [GCJ-02](#GCJ-02) and [WGS-84](/wiki/WGS-84 "WGS-84") [datums](/wiki/Geodetic_datum "Geodetic datum"). [Global Positioning System](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System") coordinates are expressed using the WGS-84 standard and when plotted on street maps of China that follow the GCJ-02 coordinates, they appear off by a large and variable amount (often over 500 metres [1,600 ft]). Authorized providers of location-based services and digital maps (such as [AutoNavi](/wiki/AutoNavi "AutoNavi"), NavInfo, or [Apple Maps](/wiki/Apple_Maps "Apple Maps")[[42]](#cite_note-43)) must purchase a "shift correction" algorithm that enables plotting GPS locations correctly on the map.[[41]](#cite_note-Corr-42) Satellite imagery and user-contributed street map data sets, such as those from [OpenStreetMap](/wiki/OpenStreetMap "OpenStreetMap") also display correctly because they have been collected using GPS devices (albeit technically illegally).

Some map providers, such as [Here](/wiki/Here_(company) "Here (company)"), choose to also offset their satellite imagery layer to match the GCJ-02 street map.[[43]](#cite_note-44)

Google has worked with Chinese location-based service provider [AutoNavi](/wiki/AutoNavi "AutoNavi") since 2006 to source its maps in China.[[44]](#cite_note-bloomberg-45)
Google uses GCJ-02 data for the street map, but does not shift the satellite imagery layer, which continues to use WGS-84 coordinates,[[45]](#cite_note-46) with the benefit that WGS-84 positions can still be overlaid correctly on the satellite image (but not the street map). Google Earth also uses WGS-84 to display the satellite imagery.[[46]](#cite_note-47)

Overlaying GPS tracks on [Google Maps](/wiki/Google_Maps "Google Maps") and any street maps sourced from Google.com via its API, will lead to a similar display offset problem, because GPS tracks use WGS-84, and Google Maps uses GCJ-02. The issue has been reported numerous times on the Google Product Forums since 2009,[[47]](#cite_note-48) with 3rd party applications emerging to fix it.[[48]](#cite_note-49) Data sets with offsets for large lists of Chinese cities existed for sale.[[49]](#cite_note-50) The problem was observed as early as 2008, and the causes were unclear, with (misguided) speculation that imported GPS chips were tampered with code that caused incorrect reporting of coordinates.[[50]](#cite_note-51)

### Hong Kong and Macau

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Restrictions_on_geographic_data_in_China&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Hong Kong and Macau")]

Under [One Country Two Systems](/wiki/One_Country_Two_Systems "One Country Two Systems"), legislation in mainland China does not apply in [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") and [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau") SARs and there are no similar restrictions in the SARs.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Therefore, the GPS shift problem does not apply. However, at the border between the SARs and mainland China, the data shown by online maps, such as Google Maps,[[51]](#cite_note-52) are broken where the shifted data and correct data overlap. This poses problems to users travelling across the border,[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] especially visitors unaware of the issue.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
